claims.text: A symposium-perspectives paper on AI/ML in Alaska Native healthcare was published May 23, 2026 in the International Journal of Circumpolar Health (doi 10.1080/22423982.2026.2677426).
claims.source_outlet: International Journal of Circumpolar Health (via PMC)

claims.text: Symposium participants by organization: ANTHC 38%, Southcentral Foundation 27%, Maniilaq Association 15%, Stanford 8%, CDC Arctic Investigation Program 8%, Other 4%.
claims.value: ANTHC 38 / SCF 27 / Maniilaq 15 / Stanford 8 / CDC-AIP 8 / Other 4
claims.notes: Include all slices so they sum to 100. Small n=31 snapshot, not a statewide statistic.

claims.text: Symposium participants by role: researchers 27%, IT/programming 24%, leadership 17%, clinicians 12%, compliance 5%, other 15%.
claims.value: researchers 27 / IT 24 / leadership 17 / clinicians 12 / compliance 5 / other 15

claims.text: Alaska's air-evacuation rates are cited as at least 40 times higher than anywhere else in the US.
Over 80% of Alaska communities are not connected to the road system.
In the 2022 baseline, 35% of respondents said they had never heard the term AI/ML.
The paper reports that overall knowledge, use, and awareness of AI/ML increased substantially between the 2022 and 2025 symposia.
claims.notes: QUALITATIVE only. Do NOT attach an invented 2025 percentage.

claims.text: The governance approach references the CARE principles (Collective Benefit, Authority to Control, Responsibility, Ethics) and the FAIR principles (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable).
The system operates under Indigenous data sovereignty, the right of a federally recognized Tribe to govern the collection, ownership, and use of its own data.
A rural tribal health organization has implemented a Cerner AI Scribe in primary care visits.
claims.notes: DEPLOYED. Cite as Cerner AI Scribe (not 'Oracle').

claims.text: ANTHC shared a statewide organizational AI use policy.
claims.notes: Governance in practice, a policy the consortium has adopted/shared.

claims.text: A medevac decision-support model is under development (proposed, not yet deployed).
claims.notes: PROPOSED / in development. Must NOT read as operational.

claims.text: The paper's authors span Southcentral Foundation, Stanford University, the Alaska Native Tribal Health Consortium, and Maniilaq Association.
claims.notes: Lead author Vanessa Hiratsuka (SCF/UAA). UAF Center for Alaska Native Health Research (Stacy Rasmus) is on the COMPANION paper only.

claims.text: A companion community-engaged AI framework paper for the Alaska Tribal Health System was published April 7, 2025 in Frontiers in Artificial Intelligence.
claims.notes: Same-team companion source (NOT fully independent). Includes UAF CANHR (Stacy Rasmus).

honesty: SINGLE-SOURCE STORY. Nearly all claims rest on one peer-reviewed paper (IJCH, 2026-05-23); the only corroboration is a same-team companion paper (Frontiers in AI, 2025-04-07), NOT independent. The deck carries an explicit single-source honesty treatment (technique 58e).
The anchor paper is ~9 weeks old (2026-05-23). Governance/context documentation, NOT breaking news. Frame accordingly and date it on-deck.

DEPLOYED vs PROPOSED must stay distinct: the Cerner AI Scribe in primary care is a real deployment (c11); the medevac decision-support model is under development / proposed (c13) and must not read as operational.
NARROW machine-learning tools, not 'general AI.' No slide may imply autonomous or general AI.
Quote figures with their qualifiers ('at least 40 times', 'over 80%'). Percentages that could not be confirmed were dropped, not approximated.
The org/role percentage breakdowns are self-reported from a 31-person symposium (small n); present as a snapshot of who convened, not a statewide statistic.
